turns.content: Hey, I've been trying to port an old C project that uses tar files over here, but I'm still fuzzy on how the crate handles streaming extraction without loading everything into memory. I read somewhere that you can use the Read trait with some wrapper, but I don't quite grasp how to actually implement this in my code.

You're mixing up a couple of things. The crate itself doesn't let you just read files as streams directly; it wraps the file handle so you can extract entries one by one using an iterator over the archive's files. You need to open your tar file, then iterate through its contents and process each entry individually without buffering the whole thing.
Wait, I thought opening a file gave me a File object that already implements Read? So why can't I just pipe it straight into something like BufReader or similar to get streaming behavior?

That's a common misunderstanding. While File does implement Read, the crate wraps that file in an Archive structure specifically so you can iterate over its entries without loading them all into memory at once. You still need to handle the file handle separately for streaming reads if your logic requires it, but the archive itself provides the iterator pattern for extracting files.

I'm trying to add a file to an archive in my Rust project, but I keep getting errors related to the impl trait for File. My background is in Python scripting where I just call functions, so I thought this would be as simple as passing a string path and letting it handle everything.

You're running into that because Rust requires explicit ownership of the File handle. The crate zip doesn't accept a String for the file path directly; you must create a temporary buffer or use std::fs::File to wrap your input before passing it to the add method on the ZipArchive. You need to implement Drop manually if you want automatic cleanup, otherwise the file stays open.

That sounds overly complicated for what I'm trying to do. Can't I just use std::fs::File like in Python and let it close automatically? I feel like I'm over-engineering this because I don't understand why Rust is forcing me to manage the lifetime of that file handle.

You're right that it feels heavy, but Rust's ownership model prevents memory leaks by ensuring resources are released when you drop a variable. When you pass a File into zip::ZipArchive, the crate takes ownership of that handle and manages its lifetime until the archive is dropped or explicitly closed with close(). If you just return the file handle without transferring ownership, it stays open in the caller's scope and never gets cleaned up.
